title: The Cast of Eva Likable or Unlikable
display_title: The Cast of Eva Likable or Unlikable
wikitext:
[]: The Cast of Eva: Likable or Unlikable?             Quote Post by Gendowned » Tue Jan 19, 2010 8:01 pm Of all the arguments I've seen criticizing the cast of Eva (more particularly the character of Shinji), the one I've found most difficult to refute is: "The characters weren't meant to be likable, so why bother defending them?" However, I've also found it a difficult argument to accept. While I understand that the characters of Eva were, no doubt, not intended to be lovable much less objects of idolization (due to their heavy flaws), what I don't understand is why the positive and negative qualities in the characters have to be mutually exclusive. The common answer to this seems to be that Anno intended the characters to be unlikable first and foremost (I have not read such a statement made by him, but I also haven't done all of my homework) and, therefore, pointing out any redeeming qualities will only render his intent pointless...but even so, does a character being written as "unlikable" mean they're required to be void of positive qualities? I hope I'm making sense here. Basically, what I'm asking is--from an objective standpoint, were characters such as Shinji and Misato intended to be likable, unlikable, or is it simply up to the audience to decide? Was there a statement made by the staff that can confirm it for us? Can such characters really be so easily sorted into slots labeled "likable" or "unlikable"? Discuss.

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Tue Jan 19, 2010 8:40 pm I don't think Anno intended it to go either way. His intention was to burn his thoughts and feelings into film. As such, the characters are a reflection of him, a broken man who ran away and was simply not dead. He meant for the characters to be dynamic, and flawed like real people. I do think that on some level, all of the principle characters are genuinely unlikeable, although I like a lot of the characters for that reason.

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Tue Jan 19, 2010 8:45 pm I would disagree with the notion that the characters in Evangelion were meant to be unlikeable. I would submit that the characters were instead meant to be flawed . The characters in Evangelion have realistic and serious flaws in their personalities. However this does not in itself mean that they were meant to be unlikeable; though we are meant to recognise their negative qualities. However I feel that in general, the flaws of the characters humanise them and indeed help endear the audience to them through sympathy. How well this works depends to an extent on the character in question. The mild mannered Shinji usually gets very little slack from the fandom, whereas the hot tempered Asuka find many supporters. I would submit that the many comic moments we see, particularly earlier in the series, are meant to make the characters likeable and edear them to us. We are shown their lighter sides so that we can sympathise with their plight later on. The cast of Evangelion is a deeply flawed one, but personally I find that is what makes them so human, and the series so compelling.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Wed Jan 20, 2010 12:48 am I would submit the query that they were designed to be flawed, causing you to be disgusted with them, then unnerved with them as you begin thinking about it, due to the similarities to the self, and then finally you're supposed to come to the conclusion of loving them, because only then can the truth of Eva be seen.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Wed Jan 20, 2010 9:37 am Well, focusing only on Shinji/adolescent characters: People seemed to be turned off to characters that remind them of themselves or people they know. I honestly don't think Shinji did anything in the TV series that wasn't to be expected of adolescence in general. It just wasn't glamorized like it is in most forms of entertainment, where it's normally shown as "the blissful days of our innocent youth". Poppycock. I'd totally react like Shinji did if, at the already awkward age of 14, I was forced to live with some smokin' hot 20-something chick who drinks a lot, and also with some strange, attractive foreign girl around my age; who probably isn't even speaking my native language well, hence her yelling and broad hand gestures; who then starts her first period surrounded by strangers in a weird culture of some foreign land. (Yeah, I'm a dude. But having three sisters learns you a lot. My older sister acted a whole lot like Asuka when she hit 13-14. Menstrual pains explained EVERYTHING.) I mean, that part of growing up isn't that very likable in the first place. So why expect realistically flawed character to have lots of "likable" moments in it? Now Shinji in EoE, on the other hand....

[]:             Quote Post by Lament » Wed Jan 20, 2010 2:52 pm The characters are indeed very flawed and that makes them realistic. However realistic doesn't always mean likable. In my case, the characters grew on me very slowly. I guess the characters are likable if you can symphathize with them. ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: The mild mannered Shinji usually gets very little slack from the fandom, whereas the hot tempered Asuka find many supporters.

[]:             Quote Post by Mad » Wed Jan 20, 2010 5:19 pm I think the main cast of NGE are "real" people. Not "I can reach out and touch you" real but depicted in a realistic view. I'll use Shinji as an example. There are times when I loved Shinji, there are times when I hated Shinji, there are times when I felt empathy, sympathy, apathy, happy for, mad at, frustrated with, jealous of, and bothered by Shinji. My previous statement can be applied to almost every character of the main cast; they were portrayed in a very realistic view, unlike many stereotyped anime heroes nowadays that are easily forgotten. So all in all, the cast is "likable' to me but in the sense that they are "real" not just because I "like" them. Get it?

[]:             Quote Post by Bomby von Bombsville » Wed Jan 20, 2010 6:51 pm The fact that all the characters are massively flawed makes them all a bit more endearing in certain moments. For example, I couldn't stand Asuka at all until the mindrape sequence. I understood her at that point. This is actually a phenomenon that I remember discussing during my freshman year of college. We were speaking on enlightenment philosophers, and my professor had brought up that if a person is at first unlikable and has a major change for the better, the person is more endearing than they would be had they been seen as likable from the start.

[]:             Quote Post by firegirl26 » Wed Jan 20, 2010 8:40 pm Mad wrote: I think the main cast of NGE are "real" people. Not "I can reach out and touch you" real but depicted in a realistic view. I'll use Shinji as an example. There are times when I loved Shinji, there are times when I hated Shinji, there are times when I felt empathy, sympathy, apathy, happy for, mad at, frustrated with, jealous of, and bothered by Shinji. My previous statement can be applied to almost every character of the main cast; they were portrayed in a very realistic view, unlike many stereotyped anime heroes nowadays that are easily forgotten. So all in all, the cast is "likable' to me but in the sense that they are "real" not just because I "like" them. Get it?

[]:             Quote Post by Ironfoot » Thu Jan 21, 2010 2:49 am Likable or unlikable, that's really up to you and your experiences with life. When I first watched this show, I found the majority I could identify with, but after many years I grew with the understanding that most were actually pretty selfish and only cared about themselves. It really depends on who you are and your viewpoint on life itself, with Eva being realistic and all.
